title: Bipartite perfect matching is in quasi-NC
speaker: Stephen Fenner
abstract: We show that the bipartite perfect matching problem is in $\textrm{quasi-}\textsf{NC}^2$. That is, it has uniform circuits of quasi-polynomial size and $O(\log^2 n)$ depth. Previously, only an exponential upper bound was known on the size of such circuits with poly-logarithmic depth. We obtain our result by an almost complete derandomization of the Isolation Lemma of Mulmuley, Vazirani, & Vazirani, used to yield an efficient randomized parallel algorithm for the bipartite perfect matching problem.

proof_stages.label: problem context and quasi-NC theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines matching and perfect matching, reviews randomized and special-case parallel algorithms, and states the joint result that bipartite perfect matching decision and search have uniform quasi-NC circuits of depth O(log^2 n) and size 2^{O(log^2 n)}.

proof_stages.label: determinant reduction using isolating weights
proof_stages.summary: For a weighted bipartite graph, the speaker encodes edges by entries 2^{w(e)} in a biadjacency matrix so that an isolating weight function makes the determinant nonzero exactly when a perfect matching exists, recalling the randomized isolation lemma as the standard RNC route.

proof_stages.label: modular candidate weights and circulation obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The proof replaces the exponentially large powers-of-two isolating weights by their residues modulo small integers and introduces cycle circulation, because two distinct minimum-weight perfect matchings can differ only along zero-circulation alternating cycles.

proof_stages.label: small family forcing nonzero circulation
proof_stages.summary: The first key lemma asserts that for any set of s cycles, one residue-modulo-j weight function with j at most n^2s gives nonzero circulation to all of them, so polynomially many short cycles can be handled using only O(log n)-bit weights.

proof_stages.label: derived-graph zero-circulation lemma
proof_stages.summary: The derived graph, defined as the union of all minimum-weight perfect matchings for a given weight, is shown in the bipartite case to have only zero-circulation cycles and no extra nonminimum perfect matchings, with a later proof via the bipartite perfect-matching polytope.

proof_stages.label: iterated elimination of short cycles
proof_stages.summary: Using the fact that a graph with no cycles up to length r has at most n^4 cycles up to length 2r, the algorithmic proof conceptually forms successive derived graphs whose girth doubles until all cycles disappear, leaving a single perfect matching.

proof_stages.label: lexicographic gluing into one isolating weight
proof_stages.summary: The stage-wise weights are combined as base-B digits in a master weight function, so the first stage at which any other perfect matching drops out gives a lexicographic weight comparison proving that the remaining matching is uniquely minimum in the original graph.

proof_stages.label: parallel exhaustive search and consequences
proof_stages.summary: Since the correct sequence of modular weights is not known, all O(log n)-round choices are tried in parallel and each resulting determinant is tested, with Chinese-remainder implementation details preserving O(log^2 n) depth; the same framework also gives an RNC algorithm using O(log^2 n) random bits and several quasi-NC corollaries via known reductions.

examples.name: alternating blue-red octagon
examples.note: The pictured octagon in the symmetric difference of two matchings illustrates how a nonzero-circulation alternating cycle would allow a swap that lowers one of two supposed minimum-weight matchings.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the blue-red octagon cycle to reason through nonzero circulation, swapping blue and red matching edges and deriving a contradiction to minimality.

examples.name: non-bipartite prism counterexample
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker gives a weighted non-bipartite prism-like graph whose derived graph contains all edges and hence a nonminimum perfect matching, showing why the derived-graph lemma is genuinely bipartite.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs the prism counterexample with specified edge weights, identifies minimum perfect matchings and a weight-three nonminimum matching, and checks how the derived graph violates the bipartite corollary.
